skill.name: building-hooks
skill.description: Create, review, and maintain Claude Code hooks for event-driven automation, validation, logging, and policy enforcement.
skill.summary: Build and validate Claude Code event hooks for automation and security policies.
skill.author: C0ntr0lledCha0s

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many critical command patterns, but review found the destructive examples are presented as commands to block rather than instructions to execute. The skill is still medium risk because it teaches and templates Claude Code hooks that execute local shell scripts and inspect untrusted tool parameters.

medium_findings.title: External Command Hooks Are Core Behavior
medium_findings.description: The skill teaches hooks that run Bash commands from hooks.json. This is legitimate for Claude Code automation, but unsafe generated or edited hooks can execute local scripts with project privileges.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The static external command findings match the documented command-hook design. The risk is real capability exposure, although it is expected behavior rather than evidence of malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Validation Hook Uses Blocklist Matching on Shell Input
medium_findings.description: The sample Bash validator reads the proposed Bash command and blocks a small set of dangerous patterns. Blocklist-only validation can miss variants, aliases, encoded commands, or multi-step destructive behavior.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The template directly processes Bash tool parameters and uses a limited regex denylist. The code quotes variables and does not directly eval input, so the concern is incomplete enforcement rather than immediate injection.

low_findings.title: Destructive Command Matches Are Defensive Examples
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged rm, dd, mkfs, chmod, wget, and curl patterns. In the reviewed context, these appear in checklists and examples that instruct users to block or avoid them.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited sections label the commands as prohibited or blocked patterns. This strongly supports a false-positive classification for the critical destructive-command alerts.

low_findings.title: Home Directory Hook Paths Are Operational Examples
low_findings.description: The skill references hook scripts under the Claude configuration directory. This is normal hook placement guidance, but users should review any script before installing it there.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The hidden home-directory paths are presented as Claude hook locations, not as secret harvesting or unauthorized access. The residual risk is that installed hooks can affect user-level configuration.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Command Hook Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The hook templates use command entries that invoke Bash scripts. This pattern is powerful and should require review, path allowlisting, and least-privilege script design.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command-hook pattern is explicit and repeated in the skill. It is a dangerous pattern by capability, even though it is an intended feature.

dangerous_patterns.title: Dangerous Command Denylist Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The validation example blocks a narrow regex set for destructive Bash commands. Denylists are useful as examples but should not be the only enforcement mechanism.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The regex blocklist is visible in the executable template. It reduces obvious risk but can be bypassed by command variants, so confidence is high enough for a medium warning.

content.value_statement: Claude Code hooks are powerful but easy to misconfigure. This skill helps create, validate, migrate, and review hooks with security-focused patterns.
actual_capabilities: Explains valid Claude Code hook events, matchers, and command hook structure.
Provides templates for PreToolUse, PostToolUse, lifecycle, and prompt-based hooks.
Guides validation of hooks.json syntax, event names, matchers, and hook fields.
Reviews hook configurations for dangerous command patterns and unsafe permissions.
Supports migration, comparison, and maintenance workflows for hook configurations.
Includes a sample Bash validation hook for blocking risky file and shell operations.
limitations: The skill does not guarantee that generated shell scripts are safe in every environment.
Hook examples require local review before use in production projects.
The validator checks structure and common mistakes, not every business policy.
Command hooks can affect files and processes when users install and run them.

use_cases.title: Standardize Team Hooks
use_cases.description: Create consistent hook templates that enforce formatting, validation, and logging across a repository.

use_cases.title: Review Risky Hook Changes
use_cases.description: Check hooks.json updates and referenced scripts for unsafe command execution and weak guardrails.

use_cases.title: Migrate Hook Configurations
use_cases.description: Update older hook configurations to current event names, matcher rules, and validation expectations.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Hook
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Claude Code hook that runs a formatter after Write and Edit tools. Explain where each file should live.

prompt_templates.title: Validate an Existing Config
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this hooks.json for schema mistakes, invalid matchers, missing fields, and unsafe command patterns.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Security Gate
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a PreToolUse Bash validation hook that blocks protected paths and warns before network commands.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Hook Migration
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare these old and new hook configurations, identify behavior changes, and propose a safe migration plan.

output_examples.input: A user asks for a hook that prevents edits under protected system directories.
output_examples.output: A policy-oriented hook plan with protected path checks, expected block behavior, test cases, and deployment notes.

output_examples.input: A team submits a hooks.json file for review before merge.
output: Schema issues are listed by event.
Security concerns are separated from warnings.
Suggested fixes are described in plain language.

output_examples.input: A project needs to migrate hooks to newer lifecycle events.
output_examples.output: A step-by-step migration checklist covering event changes, matcher removal, validation, and rollback checks.

best_practices: Review every generated command hook before enabling it in a shared repository.
Use allowlists for paths, tools, and commands instead of broad blocklists alone.
Test hooks with harmless inputs before relying on them for security enforcement.
anti_patterns: Do not run hook scripts that concatenate untrusted input into shell commands.
Do not use wildcard matchers when a hook should apply to specific tools only.
Do not store secrets or credentials inside hooks.json or hook scripts.

faq.question: What does this skill help build?
faq.answer: It helps build Claude Code hooks for validation, automation, logging, formatting, and policy enforcement.

faq.question: Can it validate hook syntax?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes guidance and a Python validator for hook event names, matchers, and required fields.

faq.question: Does it run hooks automatically?
faq.answer: No. Users must install generated hook files and enable them in their Claude Code configuration.

faq.question: Is it suitable for security policies?
faq.answer: It can help design security hooks, but each script needs local review and testing before enforcement.

faq.question: Why does the audit mention external commands?
faq.answer: Hooks are designed to execute local commands, so command execution is an expected but sensitive capability.

faq.question: Can Codex or Claude Code use this skill?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code workflows.
